title: ORDER BY — sort employees by salary
instruction:
`ORDER BY` controls the order of results. Without it, the database returns rows in an undefined order — never rely on it. Always sort explicitly when order matters.

Sort employees from highest to lowest salary.
successMessage: Eva Martinez tops the list at $195k. The range from $72k (Sales Rep) to $195k (Head of DS) spans 2.7x. Swap `DESC` for `ASC` to reverse the order.

title: Multi-column ORDER BY — sort by department, then salary
instruction:
You can sort by multiple columns. The database sorts by the first column, then breaks ties using the second column.

Sort employees by department name, then by salary within each department.
starter:
SELECT
  d.name    AS department,
  e.name    AS employee,
  e.salary
FROM employees e
JOIN departments d ON e.department_id = d.id
ORDER BY d.name ASC, e.salary DESC;
successMessage: Departments are alphabetical (ASC). Within each department, employees are ranked by salary (DESC). This is the pattern for org charts, leaderboards, and any grouped ranking.

title: LIMIT and OFFSET — top N and pagination
instruction:
`LIMIT N` returns at most N rows. `OFFSET M` skips the first M rows. Together they power pagination in web apps and 'top 10' reports.

Get the top 5 highest-paid employees — page 1 of a salary report.
starter:
-- Page 1: top 5
SELECT name, job_title, salary
FROM employees
ORDER BY salary DESC
LIMIT 5;
successMessage: The top 5 earners — all VP/Head level plus one Senior Engineer. Change `LIMIT 5` to `LIMIT 5 OFFSET 5` to get positions 6–10 (page 2). This is exactly how every paginated API endpoint works.
